html:
Choosing TPO for a Roof That Has to Survive West Texas Summers

TPO has become the default recommendation for a lot of Odessa's flat and low-slope commercial roofs, and for good reason — it reflects heat well and installs fast. But the material choice isn't the whole decision. The thickness, the seam work, and the installer's attention to detail decide whether it lasts fifteen years or fails at year six.

Why TPO Fits This Climate

Odessa buildings bake. Between the elevation, the latitude, and the lack of tree cover across most of Ector County, roof surface temperatures on a summer afternoon get brutal. TPO's white or light-colored surface reflects a meaningful share of that heat instead of absorbing it, which matters for cooling costs on office buildings downtown and for keeping equipment rooms under fabrication shop roofs from overheating.

It also handles the freeze-thaw swings better than some older membrane types. Odessa doesn't sit through long winters, but it does take sudden cold snaps, and a membrane that gets brittle in a hard freeze is a membrane that cracks the next time wind flexes it.

The Decision Owners Actually Have to Make

Not every TPO job is the same job. The thickness — usually 45, 60, or 80 mil — changes how much punishment the membrane takes before it needs attention, and the price gap between them is smaller than most owners assume relative to the building's total roof budget. A distribution building or fabrication shop with foot traffic from maintenance crews and mechanical units on the roof is a worse candidate for the thinnest membrane than a rarely-accessed office roof, even if both are technically flat.

The bigger risk isn't the mil thickness, though. It's seam quality. TPO seams are hot-air welded, and a welder who rushes the pass leaves seams that look fine on installation day and open up within a few storm seasons. We tell owners to ask any roofer bidding TPO work how they probe and document seam welds, because that's the part of the job nobody sees once the roof is done.

Where TPO Underperforms in Odessa

TPO isn't the right answer everywhere. Roofs near fabrication shop exhaust vents or anywhere with regular exposure to oils, greases, and solvents put stress on TPO that it isn't formulated to resist as well as PVC. We've seen TPO installed on pipe yard buildings where the roof looked like the cheaper, faster option, only for the owner to deal with membrane degradation near vent stacks a few years in. If your building runs hydrocarbons or heavy grease near the roof deck, that's a conversation worth having before the material gets picked.

What a Bad TPO Install Costs You Later

A poorly welded seam doesn't fail on day one. It fails during the next hailstorm or wind event, usually in the exact spot where water then finds its way to insulation and deck. By the time an owner notices a ceiling stain, the damage is often already spread wider than the visible leak suggests. That's the real cost of a rushed install — not the membrane price, but the hidden repair bill two or three years out.

  • Seam weld quality and documentation
  • Membrane thickness matched to roof traffic and equipment load
  • Insulation R-value and attachment method underneath
  • Flashing detail at parapets, curbs, and penetrations
  • Drainage slope so water doesn't sit on the membrane

How We Approach a TPO Job

We start with what the roof needs to do, not with the membrane brand. That means checking the existing deck and insulation condition on a retrofit, mapping out every roof penetration before crews mobilize, and setting a seam inspection process that catches problems before the roof is closed up. On new metal building projects along I-20 and US-385, we coordinate with the general contractor's schedule so the membrane goes down in a weather window that gives the adhesive and welds time to cure properly.

We also tell owners plainly when TPO isn't the right call. If a roof deck has moisture problems that need addressing first, or if the building's use points toward PVC or a different system, we say so before quoting TPO just because it's the easiest sale.

Common Questions About TPO in Odessa

How long should a TPO roof last here?

Service life depends heavily on membrane thickness, seam quality, and how much roof traffic the building sees. A well-installed 60 or 80 mil TPO roof with sound seams should outperform a rushed installation by a wide margin, regardless of what the warranty paperwork claims.

Is TPO a good fit for a fabrication shop or service yard building?

Often, yes, but it depends on what's happening near the roof — exhaust stacks, mechanical units, and any hydrocarbon exposure change the calculus. We look at the specific building before recommending TPO over PVC for industrial use.

Can TPO go over an existing roof, or does it need a full tear-off?

It depends on the condition of what's underneath. If the existing insulation is dry and the deck is sound, a recover can work. If there's trapped moisture, covering it with a new membrane just delays a bigger problem.

Does TPO handle hail as well as other membranes?

Thicker TPO membranes hold up reasonably well against hail compared to some alternatives, but no single-ply membrane is hail-proof. Impact resistance is one more factor in the thickness conversation, especially for buildings out toward West Odessa where hail tends to hit hardest.

What does a TPO warranty actually cover?

Manufacturer warranties typically cover membrane defects, not installation error. That's why we spend more time on installation quality control than on warranty paperwork — a warranty doesn't fix a leak while you're waiting on a claim to process.
